name: 1 · Copy values into a shape
description: The spec is a picture of the output. Objects and arrays in it are literal output structure; a string starting with $. is a path into the input document. Anything else — "crm", true, numbers — passes through verbatim. A path that finds nothing simply omits its key.

name: 2 · Text: concat, case, trim, replace, substring
description: Value functions take their data as the operation's argument and nest freely — #upper wraps a #concat here. #replace is literal, not regex, and uses #in/#find/#with. #substring uses #of/#start/#end, clamps out-of-range values, and counts negative offsets from the end.

name: 3 · Missing data, defaults, coercion
description: Missing paths omit their key rather than emitting null — see how "omitted" never appears in the output. #default supplies a fallback for any operation. #literal escapes a value so # keys inside it are treated as data, not as a spec to execute.

#literal.#stream: this is data, not a spec

name: 4 · Conditionals and lookup tables
description: #if takes a match object as its condition, with #then/#else branches that are themselves full specs. #lookup maps a value through a table with #on naming the value to look up; #default catches anything not in the table.

name: 5 · Streams: filter and map
description: #stream names the source array; stages then run over each element, where @. refers to the current element (as $. still refers to the document root). #map takes either a single path or a whole object template, so you can reshape each element.

name: 6 · Match objects: every operator
description: Predicates are plain JSON, Mongo-style. A bare value means equality; an object value applies operators, and several in one object must all hold. #and/#or/#not combine whole predicates. Right-hand sides are expressions too, so a filter can compare against a value from the document root.

name: 7 · Ordering and slicing
description: Here the elements are scalars, so "@" alone refers to each element itself rather than a field of it. #sortBy takes an element path, or an options object with #path and #order for descending. #skip and #limit paginate; #distinct is numeric-aware, treating 1 and 1.0 as equal.

name: 8 · flatMap: flatten nested arrays
description: #flatMap maps each element then flattens one level, turning an array of arrays into a flat stream. Empty arrays contribute nothing. Stages compose, so flattening can feed straight into #distinct and #sortBy.

name: 9 · Aggregates: sum, avg, min, max, count, join
description: Terminals end a pipeline and collapse the stream to one value. #sum/#avg/#min/#max take an element path and skip non-numeric values; #count/#first/#last take "@" because the source was already named by #stream. #join takes the separator string.

name: 10 · Testing a stream: any / all / none
description: These terminals answer yes/no questions about a stream and return a boolean. Note "noneSkipped": because a missing field never satisfies #exists, none of the elements match, so the answer is true.

name: 11 · groupBy
description: #groupBy collects elements into an object keyed by the extracted value, preserving each group's members. Because the result is an object, #entries turns it back into a stream of {key, value} pairs — which is how you post-process groups.

name: 12 · Terminals without a pipeline
description: When you aren't filtering or mapping anything, skip #stream entirely: the terminal takes the data directly as its argument. Available for #count, #first, #last, #distinct, #reverse, #sum, #min, #max and #avg — aggregates then operate on the elements themselves.

name: 13 · Dynamic keys: entries, toObject, spread
description: #spread copies an object's fields into the output, and keys you declare alongside it override what was copied — see how "products" replaces the input array with a keyed object. #toObject builds keys from data via #key/#value, and #entries goes the other way, turning an object into a stream of pairs.

name: 14 · Variables (#let) and chaining
description: #let computes a value once and binds it to a name; $shipped (no dot) is that variable, while $.shipped (with a dot) would be a field of the document. A spec can also be an array of steps — each step's output becomes the next step's input, so step 2 reads $.shippedRevenue produced by step 1.

name: 15 · Expressions: named, hybrid, portable
description: For logic that outgrows JSON, expressions run code. Store them in a catalog (the Expressions pane) with one entry per dialect, then reference them by name with #expr — the spec never names a dialect, so it stays portable while each runtime picks the engine it has. Inside expressions, root is the document and it is the current element. #mvel is disabled on this public instance because it executes arbitrary Java; #js runs sandboxed.
